Military unit

The **West Yorkshire Regiment (Prince of Wales's Own)** (14th Foot) was an [infantry](/wiki/Infantry "Infantry") [regiment](/wiki/Regiment "Regiment") of the [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army"). In 1958 it amalgamated with the [East Yorkshire Regiment](/wiki/East_Yorkshire_Regiment "East Yorkshire Regiment") (15th Foot) to form the [Prince of Wales's Own Regiment of Yorkshire](/wiki/Prince_of_Wales%27s_Own_Regiment_of_Yorkshire "Prince of Wales's Own Regiment of Yorkshire") which was, on 6 June 2006, amalgamated with the [Green Howards](/wiki/Green_Howards "Green Howards") and the [Duke of Wellington's Regiment (West Riding)](/wiki/Duke_of_Wellington%27s_Regiment "Duke of Wellington's Regiment") to form the [Yorkshire Regiment (14th/15th, 19th and 33rd/76th Foot)](/wiki/Royal_Yorkshire_Regiment "Royal Yorkshire Regiment").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Yorkshire_Regiment&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Formation to 1776

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Yorkshire_Regiment&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Formation to 1776")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Soldier_of_14th_regiment_1742.jpg/250px-Soldier_of_14th_regiment_1742.jpg)](/wiki/File:Soldier_of_14th_regiment_1742.jpg)

Soldier of 14th regiment, 1742

The regiment was raised by Sir [Edward Hales](/wiki/Sir_Edward_Hales,_3rd_Baronet "Sir Edward Hales, 3rd Baronet") in response to the 1685 [Monmouth Rebellion](/wiki/Monmouth_Rebellion "Monmouth Rebellion").[[3]](#cite_note-3) Following the 1688 [Glorious Revolution](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution "Glorious Revolution") and deposition of [James II](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England"), Hales was replaced as colonel by William Beveridge; after serving in Scotland, the unit was sent to [Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders") in 1693, and gained its first [battle honour](/wiki/Battle_honour "Battle honour") at [Namur](/wiki/Siege_of_Namur_(1695) "Siege of Namur (1695)") in 1695.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

After the 1697 [Treaty of Ryswick](/wiki/Treaty_of_Ryswick "Treaty of Ryswick"), the regiment served in Ireland until 1715, when it moved to [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") to take part in the [1715 Jacobite Rising](/wiki/Jacobite_rising_of_1715 "Jacobite rising of 1715"). It fought at [Glen Shiel](/wiki/Battle_of_Glen_Shiel "Battle of Glen Shiel") in 1719, before returning to England.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Posted to [Gibraltar](/wiki/Gibraltar "Gibraltar") in 1727 during the [1727 Siege](/wiki/Thirteenth_Siege_of_Gibraltar "Thirteenth Siege of Gibraltar"), it served there as part of the garrison for the next 15 years.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

During the [War of the Austrian Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Austrian_Succession "War of the Austrian Succession"), it fought at [Fontenoy](/wiki/Battle_of_Fontenoy "Battle of Fontenoy") in 1745, before being recalled to Scotland to suppress the [1745 Rebellion](/wiki/Jacobite_rising_of_1745 "Jacobite rising of 1745"), taking part in the battles of [Falkirk](/wiki/Battle_of_Falkirk_Muir "Battle of Falkirk Muir") and [Culloden](/wiki/Battle_of_Culloden "Battle of Culloden").[[7]](#cite_note-7) Following the reforms of 1751, it became the 14th Regiment of Foot, then returned to Gibraltar for another 8-year stay.[[8]](#cite_note-8) In 1765, when stationed at Windsor, it was granted royal permission for the grenadiers to wear bearskin caps with the [White Horse of Hanover](/wiki/White_Horse_of_Hanover "White Horse of Hanover") signifying the favour of the King.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

In 1766, the regiment left [Portsmouth](/wiki/Portsmouth "Portsmouth") for [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America") and was stationed in [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia").[[10]](#cite_note-cannon34-10)

### American War of Independence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Yorkshire_Regiment&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: American War of Independence")]

Although part of the city garrison, the 14th was not involved in the [Boston Massacre](/wiki/Boston_Massacre "Boston Massacre"). Captain Thomas (29th Foot) was the officer of the day in charge of the duty detail (29th of Foot) that faced the crowds outside of the Customs House. The crowd that gathered began taunting the detail until a shot, then volley was fired into the crowd, three civilians were killed outright and two more died later. Captain Preston and the detail went to trial and were successfully defended by Lawyer [John Adams](/wiki/John_Adams "John Adams") thus ending tensions between the crown and the citizens of Boston for the time being.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/14th_Foot_colours.jpg/250px-14th_Foot_colours.jpg)](/wiki/File:14th_Foot_colours.jpg)

Regimental colours, 1845

The 14th remained in Boston until 1772, when it was sent to [St Vincent](/wiki/Saint_Vincent_(Antilles) "Saint Vincent (Antilles)") in the Caribbean to help suppress a [maroon](/wiki/Maroon_(people) "Maroon (people)") rebellion. By 1774, losses caused by fighting and disease meant it was scheduled to return to England; due to the rising tensions in the colonies, it was instead redeployed piecemeal to [St. Augustine, Florida](/wiki/St._Augustine,_Florida "St. Augustine, Florida") and [Providence Island](/wiki/Providence_Island_colony "Providence Island colony") in the [Bahamas](/wiki/Bahamas "Bahamas").[[10]](#cite_note-cannon34-10)

In January 1776, the 14th was part of the amphibious expedition that took part in the [burning of Norfolk, Virginia](/wiki/Burning_of_Norfolk "Burning of Norfolk").[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERussell200074-12) In August, the fleet returned to New York, where the remnants of the 14th were used to supplement other units, while its officers went back to Britain to recruit a new regiment.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

In 1777, one company each from the newly formed 14th and the [15th](/wiki/East_Yorkshire_Regiment "East Yorkshire Regiment") regiments were sent to America under Colonel [Patrick Ferguson](/wiki/Patrick_Ferguson "Patrick Ferguson") to test the concept of the [rifle company](/wiki/Company_(military_unit)#British_Army "Company (military unit)"). These fought at the [battle of Brandywine](/wiki/Battle_of_Brandywine "Battle of Brandywine") on 11 September; after returning to England, they became the [light companies](/wiki/History_of_British_light_infantry "History of British light infantry") of their respective regiments.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

### The French Wars

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Yorkshire_Regiment&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: The French Wars")]

In 1782, the 14th was named **The 14th (Bedfordshire) Regiment**. The outbreak of the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution") and the subsequent French invasion of the Low Countries led to a British force commanded by the Duke of York being sent to join troops of the Imperial Austrian army. The 14th distinguished themselves in numerous actions, at Famars and Valenciennes in 1793 and at Tournai in 1794, for which they were subsequently granted the battle honour 'Tournay'. At the [Battle of Famars](/wiki/Battle_of_Famars "Battle of Famars"), in order to encourage the men, Lieutenant-Colonel [Welbore Ellis Doyle](/wiki/Welbore_Ellis_Doyle "Welbore Ellis Doyle"), the commanding officer, ordered the band of the 14th to play the French revolutionary song “Ça Ira”.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

This was subsequently chosen as the Regimental march. In the final, unsuccessful attempt to check the French invasion of the Netherlands, the 14th also suffered heavy casualties in the hard-fought rearguard action at Geldermalsen on 8 January 1795. There followed the disastrous winter retreat into Germany. Returning to England the following May, the Regiment was then posted to the West Indies, where it was on duty until 1803. In February 1797, the regiment participated in the bloodless [invasion of Trinidad](/wiki/Invasion_of_Trinidad_(1797) "Invasion of Trinidad (1797)").[[16]](#cite_note-16)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/14th_Foot_uniform.jpg/250px-14th_Foot_uniform.jpg)](/wiki/File:14th_Foot_uniform.jpg)

Regimental uniform, 1845

The outbreak of the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars") in 1803 led to the expansion of the [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army"). The 14th formed a second battalion in [Belfast](/wiki/Belfast "Belfast") in 1804, and a third battalion in 1813. The 1st Battalion spent much of the war on garrison duty in Bengal. In 1809, the Regiment was re-titled **The 14th (Buckinghamshire) Regiment**.[[17]](#cite_note-regiments-17) The 1st Battalion served in India for 25 years until 1831. During this period, the 1st Battalion took part in campaigns against the French in Mauritius in 1810, and the Dutch in Java in 1811, with Java adding another Battle Honour.[[18]](#cite_note-18) It took part in the [second Sambas expedition](/wiki/Sambas_expeditions "Sambas expeditions") in June 1813 against the [Sultanate of Sambas](/wiki/Sultanate_of_Sambas "Sultanate of Sambas") along with 600 [Sepoy](/wiki/Sepoy "Sepoy") troops of the [Bengal Army](/wiki/Bengal_Army "Bengal Army"), capturing the town of [Sambas](/wiki/Sambas_(town) "Sambas (town)").[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Between 1808 and 1809, the 2nd Battalion joined the [Peninsular Army](/wiki/Peninsular_War "Peninsular War") and gained the [Battle honour](/wiki/Battle_honour "Battle honour") [Corunna](/wiki/Battle_of_Corunna "Battle of Corunna").[[20]](#cite_note-20) The 2nd Battalion saw service in the [Walcheren Campaign](/wiki/Walcheren_Campaign "Walcheren Campaign") and was disbanded in 1817.[[21]](#cite_note-21) The 3rd Battalion fought at the [Battle of Waterloo](/wiki/Battle_of_Waterloo "Battle of Waterloo") in 1815; it was disbanded in 1816.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

### The Victorian era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Yorkshire_Regiment&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: The Victorian era")]

The 14th was then posted to the West Indies, Canada and Malta. In 1855, the Regiment served in the [Crimean War](/wiki/Crimean_War "Crimean War"). In 1876, the [Prince of Wales](/wiki/Edward_VII "Edward VII") presented new Colours to the 1st Battalion and conferred on the 14th the honoured title of **The Prince of Wales's Own**. A second battalion was again raised in 1858 and took part in the [New Zealand Wars](/wiki/New_Zealand_Wars "New Zealand Wars") and the [Second Anglo-Afghan War](/wiki/Second_Anglo-Afghan_War "Second Anglo-Afghan War").[[23]](#cite_note-23)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/ALEXANDER%281857%29_British_lines_under_fire.jpg/250px-ALEXANDER%281857%29_British_lines_under_fire.jpg)](/wiki/File:ALEXANDER(1857)_British_lines_under_fire.jpg)

British lines (Buckinghamshire Regiment) under fire. Illustration to the [Crimean War](/wiki/Crimean_War "Crimean War") by James E. Alexander.

The regiment was not fundamentally affected by the [Cardwell Reforms](/wiki/Cardwell_Reforms "Cardwell Reforms") of the 1870s, which gave it a depot at [Bradford Moor Barracks](/wiki/Bradford_Moor_Barracks "Bradford Moor Barracks") from 1873, or by the [Childers reforms](/wiki/Childers_reforms "Childers reforms") of 1881 – as it already possessed two battalions, there was no need for it to amalgamate with another regiment.[[24]](#cite_note-24) The regiment moved to [Imphal Barracks](/wiki/Imphal_Barracks "Imphal Barracks") in [York](/wiki/York "York") in 1878.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Under the reforms the regiment became **The Prince of Wales's Own (West Yorkshire Regiment)** on 1 July 1881.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

Between 1895 and 1896 the 2nd Battalion served in the [Gold Coast](/wiki/Gold_Coast_(British_colony) "Gold Coast (British colony)") and took part in the [Fourth Ashanti War](/wiki/Anglo-Ashanti_wars#Fourth_Anglo-Ashanti_War_(1895–1896) "Anglo-Ashanti wars").[[27]](#cite_note-27)

### Second Boer War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Yorkshire_Regiment&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Second Boer War")]

1899 saw the 2nd Battalion sent to the [Second Boer War 1899–1902](/wiki/Second_Boer_War "Second Boer War") in South Africa and after a number of engagements two members of the Battalion were awarded the Victoria Cross: Captain (later Colonel) [Mansel-Jones](/wiki/Conwyn_Mansel-Jones "Conwyn Mansel-Jones") in February 1900,[[28]](#cite_note-28) and Sergeant [Traynor](/wiki/William_Bernard_Traynor "William Bernard Traynor") in February 1901.[[29]](#cite_note-29) The 4th ([Militia](/wiki/Militia_(United_Kingdom) "Militia (United Kingdom)")) Battalion was embodied in December 1899, and 500 officers and men left for South Africa in February 1900.[[30]](#cite_note-30) The 1st, 2nd and 3rd Volunteer Battalions sent service companies to the Boer War and were granted the battle honour **South Africa 1900–02**.[[31]](#cite_note-archive1-31)[[32]](#cite_note-archive2-32)

The 3rd (Militia) Battalion was embodied on 4 May 1900, and served 14 months at [Malta](/wiki/Malta "Malta") before being stationed at [Chatham](/wiki/Chatham,_Kent "Chatham, Kent") during autumn 1901. The battalion disembodied on 1 October 1902.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

### Early 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Yorkshire_Regiment&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Early 20th century")]

In 1908, the Volunteers and Militia were reorganised nationally, with the former becoming the [Territorial Force](/wiki/Territorial_Force "Territorial Force") and the latter the [Special Reserve](/wiki/Special_Reserve_(militia) "Special Reserve (militia)");[[34]](#cite_note-34) the regiment now had two Reserve and four Territorial battalions:

* 1st (V) Bn became 5th Bn (TF), with RHQ at [Colliergate](/wiki/Colliergate_drill_hall "Colliergate drill hall") in [York](/wiki/York "York").[[35]](#cite_note-trail-35)[[36]](#cite_note-archive3-36)
* 2nd (V) Bn became [6th Bn](/wiki/Bradford_Rifles "Bradford Rifles") (TF), with RHQ at Belle Vue Barracks in [Bradford](/wiki/Bradford "Bradford") (since demolished).[[31]](#cite_note-archive1-31)[[36]](#cite_note-archive3-36)
* 3rd (V) Bn became 7th and 8th ([Leeds Rifles](/wiki/Leeds_Rifles "Leeds Rifles")) Bns (TF), a double battalion with RHQ at [Carlton Barracks](/wiki/Carlton_Barracks "Carlton Barracks") in [Leeds](/wiki/Leeds "Leeds").[[32]](#cite_note-archive2-32)[[36]](#cite_note-archive3-36)

### First World War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Yorkshire_Regiment&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: First World War")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Bradford_Pals.jpg/250px-Bradford_Pals.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bradford_Pals.jpg)

Memorial to the men of the 16th Battalion and the 18th Battalion of the regiment who died in the First World War

#### Regular Army

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Yorkshire_Regiment&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Regular Army")]

The 1st Battalion landed at [Saint-Nazaire](/wiki/Saint-Nazaire "Saint-Nazaire") as part of the [18th Brigade](/wiki/18th_Infantry_Brigade_(United_Kingdom) "18th Infantry Brigade (United Kingdom)") in the [6th Division](/wiki/6th_Infantry_Division_(United_Kingdom) "6th Infantry Division (United Kingdom)") in September 1914 for service on the [Western Front](/wiki/Western_Front_(World_War_I) "Western Front (World War I)").[[35]](#cite_note-trail-35)

The 2nd Battalion landed at [Le Havre](/wiki/Le_Havre "Le Havre") as part of the [23rd Brigade](/wiki/23rd_Infantry_Brigade_(United_Kingdom) "23rd Infantry Brigade (United Kingdom)") in the [8th Division](/wiki/8th_Division_(United_Kingdom)_in_the_First_World_War "8th Division (United Kingdom) in the First World War") in November 1914 also for service on the Western Front.[[35]](#cite_note-trail-35)

#### Territorial Force

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Yorkshire_Regiment&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Territorial Force")]

The 1/5th, 1/6th, 1/7th and 1/8th battalions landed at [Boulogne-sur-Mer](/wiki/Boulogne-sur-Mer "Boulogne-sur-Mer") as part of the [West Riding Brigade](/wiki/146th_Infantry_Brigade_(United_Kingdom) "146th Infantry Brigade (United Kingdom)") in the [West Riding Division](/wiki/49th_(West_Riding)_Infantry_Division "49th (West Riding) Infantry Division") in April 1915 also for service on the Western Front.[[35]](#cite_note-trail-35)

The 2/5th, 2/6th, 2/7th and 2/8th battalions landed at Le Havre as part of the [185th (2/1st West Riding) Brigade](/wiki/185th_(2/1st_West_Riding)_Brigade "185th (2/1st West Riding) Brigade") in the [62nd (2nd West Riding) Division](/wiki/62nd_(2nd_West_Riding)_Division "62nd (2nd West Riding) Division") in January 1917 also for service on the Western Front.[[35]](#cite_note-trail-35)

#### New Armies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Yorkshire_Regiment&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: New Armies")]

The 9th (Service) Battalion landed at [Suvla Bay](/wiki/Suvla "Suvla") in [Gallipoli](/wiki/Gallipoli "Gallipoli") as part of the [32nd Brigade](/wiki/32nd_Infantry_Brigade_(United_Kingdom) "32nd Infantry Brigade (United Kingdom)") in the [11th (Northern) Division](/wiki/11th_(Northern)_Division "11th (Northern) Division") in August 1915; the battalion was evacuated from Gallipoli in January 1916 and landed in [Marseille](/wiki/Marseille "Marseille") in July 1916 for service on the Western Front.[[35]](#cite_note-trail-35)

The 10th (Service) Battalion landed at Boulogne-sur-Mer as part of the [50th Brigade](/wiki/50th_Brigade_(United_Kingdom) "50th Brigade (United Kingdom)") in the [17th (Northern) Division](/wiki/17th_(Northern)_Division "17th (Northern) Division") in July 1915 for service on the Western Front.[[35]](#cite_note-trail-35)

The 11th (Service) Battalion landed at Le Havre as part of the [69th Brigade](/wiki/69th_Infantry_Brigade_(United_Kingdom) "69th Infantry Brigade (United Kingdom)") in the [23rd Division](/wiki/23rd_Division_(United_Kingdom) "23rd Division (United Kingdom)") in August 1915 for service on the Western Front and then transferred to Italy in November 1917.[[35]](#cite_note-trail-35)

The 12th (Service) Battalion landed at Le Havre as part of the [63rd Infantry Brigade](/wiki/63rd_Brigade_(United_Kingdom) "63rd Brigade (United Kingdom)") in the [21st Division](/wiki/21st_Division_(United_Kingdom) "21st Division (United Kingdom)") in September 1915 also for service on the Western Front.[[35]](#cite_note-trail-35)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/The_British_Army_on_the_Western_Front%2C_1914-1918_Q8441.jpg/250px-The_British_Army_on_the_Western_Front%2C_1914-1918_Q8441.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_British_Army_on_the_Western_Front,_1914-1918_Q8441.jpg)

Men of a [pioneer](/wiki/Pioneer_(military) "Pioneer (military)") battalion of the West Yorkshire Regiment, possibly the 21st (Service) Battalion (Wool Textile Pioneers), having a meal in a shell hole on the roadside near [Ypres](/wiki/Ypres "Ypres"), [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"), 23 December 1917.

The 15th (Service) Battalion (1st Leeds), raised by the Lord Mayor and City of [Leeds](/wiki/Leeds "Leeds"), and the 16th (Service) Battalion (1st Bradford), raised by the Lord Mayor and City of [Bradford](/wiki/Bradford "Bradford"), landed in Egypt as part of the [93rd Brigade](/wiki/93rd_Brigade_(United_Kingdom) "93rd Brigade (United Kingdom)") in the [31st Division](/wiki/31st_Division_(United_Kingdom) "31st Division (United Kingdom)") in December 1915 and then moved to France in March 1916 for service on the Western Front.[[35]](#cite_note-trail-35)

The 17th (Service) Battalion (2nd Leeds), raised by the Lord Mayor and City of Leeds, landed at Le Havre as part of the [106th Brigade](/wiki/106th_Brigade_(United_Kingdom) "106th Brigade (United Kingdom)") in the [35th Division](/wiki/35th_Division_(United_Kingdom) "35th Division (United Kingdom)") in February 1916 for service on the Western Front.[[35]](#cite_note-trail-35)

The 18th (Service) Battalion (2nd Bradford), raised by the Lord Mayor and City of Bradford, landed in Egypt as part of the [93rd Brigade](/wiki/93rd_Brigade_(United_Kingdom) "93rd Brigade (United Kingdom)") in the [31st Division](/wiki/31st_Division_(United_Kingdom) "31st Division (United Kingdom)") in December 1915 and then moved to France in March 1916 for service on the Western Front.[[35]](#cite_note-trail-35)

The 21st (Service) Battalion (Wool Textile Pioneers) landed in France as [pioneer](/wiki/Pioneer_(military) "Pioneer (military)") battalion to the [4th Division](/wiki/4th_Infantry_Division_(United_Kingdom) "4th Infantry Division (United Kingdom)") in June 1916 also for service on the Western Front.[[35]](#cite_note-trail-35)

### Inter-war years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Yorkshire_Regiment&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Inter-war years")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Governor_of_Bermuda_Lieutenant-General_Sir_Louis_Bols_takes_salute_at_Prospect_Camp_in_1930.jpg/250px-Governor_of_Bermuda_Lieutenant-General_Sir_Louis_Bols_takes_salute_at_Prospect_Camp_in_1930.jpg)](/wiki/File:Governor_of_Bermuda_Lieutenant-General_Sir_Louis_Bols_takes_salute_at_Prospect_Camp_in_1930.jpg)

[Governor and Commander-in-Chief of Bermuda](/wiki/Governor_of_Bermuda "Governor of Bermuda") Lieutenant-General Sir [Louis Bols](/wiki/Louis_Bols "Louis Bols") takes salute at [Prospect Camp](/wiki/Prospect_Camp,_Bermuda "Prospect Camp, Bermuda") in 1930 (a Wing of the 1st Battalion was posted to the [Bermuda Garrison](/wiki/Bermuda_Garrison "Bermuda Garrison") from 1929 to 1931).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Soldiers_of_the_1st_Battalion_of_the_West_Yorkshire_Regiment_%28Prince_of_Wales%27s_Own%29_in_Egypt_1931-1933.jpg/250px-Soldiers_of_the_1st_Battalion_of_the_West_Yorkshire_Regiment_%28Prince_of_Wales%27s_Own%29_in_Egypt_1931-1933.jpg)](/wiki/File:Soldiers_of_the_1st_Battalion_of_the_West_Yorkshire_Regiment_(Prince_of_Wales%27s_Own)_in_Egypt_1931-1933.jpg)

1st Battalion soldiers in Egypt, 1931-1933

A company of the 1st Battalion was posted to the [Imperial fortress](/wiki/Imperial_fortress "Imperial fortress") [colony](/wiki/British_Overseas_Territory "British Overseas Territory") of [Bermuda](/wiki/Bermuda "Bermuda") from 1929, relieving the 2nd Battalion, Argyll & Sutherland Highlanders, to 1931, when it was relieved by a company, 1st Battalion, Northumberland Fusiliers.[[38]](#cite_note-38) This was at a time of significant cut backs to the British Army during the interwar period that saw the saw the disbandment or reduction of units such as the [West India Regiment](/wiki/West_India_Regiments "West India Regiments") and [Royal Guernsey Militia](/wiki/Royal_Guernsey_Militia "Royal Guernsey Militia"), while responsibility for coastal defence in Britain was transferred wholly to the Territorial Army. Garrisons throughout the Empire were removed or reduced. In the Bermuda Garrison, which could not be removed entirely given the colony's role as an Imperial fortress, the regular artillery and engineering companies were removed, with their duties transferred to reservists, while the regular infantry battalion was reduced to a company detached from whichever battalion was posted to Jamaica. Reservists again shouldered the additional responsibilities. The company in Bermuda rejoined the rest of the battalion on its return to Britain from Jamaica aboard the troopship *"Lancashire"*. The unit would have a brief stay, disembarking briefly while other personnel were delivered to Southampton, but not permitted to leave the dock before reboarding for transport to its new station, Egypt.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

In 1936 the [8th (Leeds Rifles) Battalion](/wiki/Leeds_Rifles "Leeds Rifles") transferred to the [Royal Artillery](/wiki/Royal_Artillery "Royal Artillery") as 66th (Leeds Rifles, The West Yorkshire Regiment) Anti-Aircraft Brigade.[[32]](#cite_note-archive2-32)[[40]](#cite_note-40)

In 1937, the 6th Battalion became [49th (The West Yorkshire Regiment) Anti-Aircraft Battalion](/wiki/Bradford_Rifles "Bradford Rifles") of the [Royal Engineers](/wiki/Royal_Engineers "Royal Engineers"), converting to a searchlight regiment of the Royal Artillery in 1940.[[31]](#cite_note-archive1-31)[[41]](#cite_note-41)

In April 1938, the [7th (Leeds Rifles) Battalion](/wiki/Leeds_Rifles "Leeds Rifles") converted to the armoured role as [45th (Leeds Rifles) Bn, Royal Tank Regiment](/wiki/45th_(Leeds_Rifles)_Royal_Tank_Regiment "45th (Leeds Rifles) Royal Tank Regiment"). In June 1939, the company at [Morley](/wiki/Morley,_West_Yorkshire "Morley, West Yorkshire") was split off to form the cadre for a duplicate unit, the [51st (Leeds Rifles) Bn, Royal Tank Regiment](/wiki/51st_(Leeds_Rifles)_Royal_Tank_Regiment "51st (Leeds Rifles) Royal Tank Regiment").[[32]](#cite_note-archive2-32)[[42]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-42)

### Second World War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Yorkshire_Regiment&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Second World War")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/The_War_in_the_Far_East-_the_Burma_Campaign_1941-1945_IND3469.jpg/250px-The_War_in_the_Far_East-_the_Burma_Campaign_1941-1945_IND3469.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_War_in_the_Far_East-_the_Burma_Campaign_1941-1945_IND3469.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/1st_West_Yorkshire_Regiment_near_Meiktila.jpg/250px-1st_West_Yorkshire_Regiment_near_Meiktila.jpg)](/wiki/File:1st_West_Yorkshire_Regiment_near_Meiktila.jpg)

Men of the West Yorkshire Regiment search Japanese dugouts in Meiktila, 28 February 1945.

Both the 1st and 2nd battalions of the West Yorks served in the [Far East](/wiki/Far_East "Far East") throughout the [Burma Campaign](/wiki/Burma_Campaign "Burma Campaign"), fighting in the [British Fourteenth Army](/wiki/Fourteenth_Army_(United_Kingdom) "Fourteenth Army (United Kingdom)"). The 2nd Battalion served with the [9th Indian Infantry Brigade](/wiki/9th_Indian_Infantry_Brigade "9th Indian Infantry Brigade") from November 1940.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

In 1942, 2/5th Battalion West Yorkshire Regiment was converted to armour, becoming [113th Regiment Royal Armoured Corps](/w/index.php?title=113th_Regiment_Royal_Armoured_Corps&action=edit&redlink=1 "113th Regiment Royal Armoured Corps (page does not exist)"). As with all infantry battalions converted in this way, they continued to wear their West Yorkshire cap badge on the black beret of the RAC.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

The 11th Battalion formed a garrison in the [Falkland Islands](/wiki/Falkland_Islands "Falkland Islands") from June 1942 to January 1944, when it was relieved by troops of the [Royal Scots](/wiki/Royal_Scots "Royal Scots").[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47)

[51st (Leeds Rifles) Royal Tank Regiment](/wiki/51st_(Leeds_Rifles)_Royal_Tank_Regiment "51st (Leeds Rifles) Royal Tank Regiment"), formed as a 2nd Line duplicate of [45th (Leeds Rifles) Royal Tank Regiment](/wiki/45th_(Leeds_Rifles)_Royal_Tank_Regiment "45th (Leeds Rifles) Royal Tank Regiment") (previously the 7th ([Leeds Rifles](/wiki/Leeds_Rifles "Leeds Rifles")) Battalion of the West Yorks), served in [25th Army Tank Brigade](/wiki/25th_Army_Tank_Brigade "25th Army Tank Brigade") in the Italian campaign under the command of [Brigadier](/wiki/Brigadier_(United_Kingdom) "Brigadier (United Kingdom)") [Noel Tetley](/wiki/James_Noel_Tetley "James Noel Tetley") of the Leeds Rifles, who was the only Territorial Army RTR officer to command a brigade on active service. The regiment distinguished itself in support of the [1st Canadian Infantry Division](/wiki/1st_Canadian_Division "1st Canadian Division") in the assault on the [Hitler Line](/wiki/Hitler_Line "Hitler Line") in May 1944. At the request of the Canadians, 51 RTR adopted the Maple Leaf as an additional badge, which is still worn by its successors, the Leeds Detachment (Leeds Rifles), Imphal (PWO) Company, The East and West Riding Regiment.[[42]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-42)

### Postwar years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Yorkshire_Regiment&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Postwar years")]

In 1948, the 1st and 2nd Battalions were amalgamated and were stationed in Austria. They then moved to Egypt and on to Malaya. After a tour of duty in [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland") in 1955–56, the 1st Battalion took part in the [Suez Operation](/wiki/Suez_Crisis "Suez Crisis") and was then stationed in [Dover](/wiki/Dover "Dover") until the amalgamation in July 1958.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

In 1956, the merged 45th/51st (Leeds Rifles) RTR returned to the infantry role as 7th (Leeds Rifles) Bn West Yorkshire Regt and in 1961 it re-absorbed the 466th (Leeds Rifles) Light Anti-Aircraft Regt, RA, to form The Leeds Rifles, The Prince of Wales's Own Regiment of Yorkshire.[[32]](#cite_note-archive2-32)
